did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a textured scale-based approach for the automatic classification of melanocytic skin lesions in dermoscopy images. Specifically, it employs Bidimensional Empirical Mode Decomposition (BEMD) for image decomposition, Local Binary Pattern Variance (LBPV) for feature extraction, and the AdaBoost algorithm as the classifier to distinguish between melanoma and benign lesions. Post-processing refinement using color and differential structures was also applied.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents a novel textured scale-based approach for the automatic classification of melanocytic skin lesions. It details original experimental methodology including Bidimensional Empirical Mode Decomposition (BEMD) and Local Binary Pattern Variance (LBPV), describes the construction of training models using specific datasets (Derm101, PCDS, Hosei), and reports original quantitative results (Sensitivity, Specificity, Accuracy) from experiments and cross-validation. It is not a review or survey of prior literature.

title: A Textured Scale-based Approach to Melanocytic Skin Lesions in Dermoscopy
brief_summary: This study presents a novel textured scale-based approach for the automatic classification of melanocytic skin lesions in dermoscopy images to assist in melanoma detection. The methodology employs Bidimensional Empirical Mode Decomposition (BEMD) for image decomposition and Local Binary Pattern Variance (LBPV) histograms for feature extraction, followed by an AdaBoost classifier. The system was evaluated using images from three heterogeneous databases: Derm101, PCDS, and Hosei. Initial classification results achieved a sensitivity of 92.39%, specificity of 87.96%, and accuracy of 90.00% on a 200-image test set. After applying a post-processing refinement based on color count and reticular pattern detection, the performance improved to 97.83% sensitivity, 94.44% specificity, and 96.00% accuracy. The results demonstrate that the proposed method is robust and effective for melanoma classification without requiring prior lesion segmentation or artifact removal.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for detection and classification. The study focuses on distinguishing melanomas from benign lesions. Ground truth labels were assigned by dermatologists.

The comparator class in the binary classification task. The study explicitly states the task is a 'binary classification: melanoma or benign lesion'. The dataset included benign lesions (e.g., 38 benign lesions in Exp 1, 38 in Exp 2, 25 in Exp 3).

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic category (melanoma vs. benign lesion)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification to distinguish between melanoma and benign melanocytic lesions using dermoscopy images. The AdaBoost classifier predicts discrete labels (M-MELANOMA or NM-NON MELANOMA). Performance is evaluated using Sensitivity, Specificity, and Accuracy metrics on a test set of 200 images.

datasets_used.name: Derm101
datasets_used.description: A public online database of dermoscopy images used for constructing training models and testing.

use.training: Used to construct training models (images from Derm101 and PCDS were used for training).
use.validation: Used for validation (part of the 240 RGB images used for validation).
use.testing: Used for testing (part of the 200 RGB images used for testing).

datasets_used.name: PCDS (Primary Care Dermatology Society)
datasets_used.description: A public online database of dermoscopy images used for constructing training models and testing.

use.training: Used to construct training models (images from Derm101 and PCDS were used for training).
use.validation: Used for validation (part of the 240 RGB images used for validation).
use.testing: Used for testing (part of the 200 RGB images used for testing).

datasets_used.name: Hosei Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public online database of dermoscopy images used to test and evaluate the discrimination power of the proposed approach.

use.validation: Used for validation (part of the 240 RGB images used for validation).
use.testing: Used for testing (part of the 200 RGB images used for testing).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the robustness and performance of the classification model on a validation dataset of 240 images.
To divide the dataset into ten subsets with approximately equal numbers of melanomas and benign lesions for training and testing the classifier.
To compute and report the performance metrics of the melanoma classification approach.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using Sensitivity, Specificity, and Accuracy metrics, and compared qualitatively with other studies in the literature.

Derm101, PCDS, and Hosei Datasets (Combi.notes: Results are based on a test set of 200 images for experiments 1-3 and a validation set of 240 images for cross-validation. Post-processing involved refining results using color count and reticular pattern detection. The best performance was achieved in Experiment 2 with post-processing (SE=97.83%, SP=94.44%, ACC=96.00%).

architecture_used.main_model: Textured scale-based approach using Bidimensional Empirical Mode Decomposition (BEMD) for decomposition and Local Binary Pattern Variance (LBPV) for feature extraction, followed by an AdaBoost classifier.
architecture_used.input_size: 512x512 pixels (RGB images converted to gray-level and cropped/resized)

architecture_details.feature_parameters: LBP parameters (P, R) used: (8,1), (16,3), and (24,5); (24,5) achieved best results
architecture_details.imf_processing: Image decomposed into three 2D IMFs; first IMF (noise/artifacts) discarded; second and third IMFs summed to create new 2D IMF image for LBPV extraction
architecture_details.classifier: AdaBoost algorithm (selected for simultaneous feature selection and training)

classification_or_output_head.post_processing: Refinement using color count (based on ABCD rule colors: light brown, black, blue, red, dark brown, white) and reticular pattern detection (using Curvelet transform)

preprocessing_techniques: Conversion from RGB to gray-level image by selecting the highest entropy channel
Cropping operation to reduce background interference
Resizing to a fixed resolution of 512x512 pixels
Bidimensional Empirical Mode Decomposition (BEMD) for image decomposition
Discarding the first 2D IMF (containing noise and artifacts)
Summing the second and third 2D IMFs to create a new 2D IMF image
Post-processing refinement using color count (based on ABCD rule colors) and reticular pattern detection (using Curvelet transform)
conclusions: The proposed textured scale-based approach using Bidimensional Empirical Mode Decomposition (BEMD) and Local Binary Pattern Variance (LBPV) is robust and effective for classifying melanocytic skin lesions in dermoscopy images.
The method allows for the development of a Computer Aided Diagnosis (CAD) system that does not require prior lesion segmentation or artifact removal, simplifying the preprocessing pipeline.
The system demonstrates generalization capability by achieving good performance on images from three heterogeneous databases (Derm101, PCDS, and Hosei) acquired under diverse conditions.

Post-processing refinement using color count and reticular pattern detection significantly improves classification performance, achieving a sensitivity of 97.83%, specificity of 94.44%, and accuracy of 96.00%.
The choice of training set composition influences performance; specifically, increasing the number of melanoma images in the training set was observed to decrease specificity.

The approach outperformed or matched several existing texture-based methods in the literature, particularly showing improved results compared to Situ et al. in accuracy and Barata et al. in specificity when evaluated via cross-validation.
important_remarks: The study relies on a small training set size (maximum 70 images), raising concerns about generalizability and overfitting despite the use of cross-validation.
Performance is highly sensitive to training set composition; increasing the number of melanoma images in the training set was observed to decrease specificity.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) for the comparison with other methods, relying instead on qualitative comparisons of metrics.

External validation is limited to three specific public databases (Derm101, PCDS, Hosei); results may not generalize to other clinical populations or imaging devices not represented in these datasets.
The approach avoids lesion segmentation and artifact removal, which is a strength for clinical deployment simplicity, but may introduce noise or bias if artifacts correlate with lesion classes.

The post-processing refinement significantly boosts performance (e.g., Accuracy from 90.00% to 96.00% in Exp 2), suggesting the initial texture-based classifier alone is insufficient for high-accuracy clinical use.
Ground truth labels were assigned by dermatologists, but the paper does not specify inter-observer agreement or the specific criteria used for labeling, which could introduce label noise.
The comparison with other studies is complicated by heterogeneous datasets and non-uniform assessment metrics across the literature, making direct performance benchmarking difficult.
